payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
I've written project proposals in this industry using six acronyms in two sentences. I knew the reader already knew the acronyms. The writing wasn't for them. It was for the version of me that needed to feel credentialed in front of someone whose approval I wanted.

That instinct is the thing this Note is about.

There's a pattern in how the music industry writes about itself, and once you see it, it's hard to unsee. Trade press written for other trade press. Conference panels written for the people on the next panel. White papers written for the consultants who'll cite them. Podcast episodes recorded for the small group of people who already know the acronyms.

It's a closed loop. The audience the writers have in mind isn't the listener. It isn't the working artist. It isn't the curious journalist outside the music vertical. It's the same fifty people in the same rooms talking to each other in a shared dialect.

This isn't a complaint about insider language. Every industry has its own jargon, and shorthand is useful when you're moving fast among people who share context. The problem is what happens when the shorthand becomes the only register the industry can write in.

What the incentives actually reward

I want to be careful here, because the easy version of this critique points fingers at individuals. That's not where the problem lives. The problem lives in the incentive structure that the writing happens inside.

Writers who use insider dialect get invited to panels. Writers who don't, don't. Writers who name-drop the right acronyms get cited in the right trade publications. Writers who stop and explain the acronym for the broader reader get treated as less serious by their peers, even when their writing is more useful to anyone outside the room.

The result isn't that any individual writer is acting in bad faith. The result is a field where the rational career move is to write inaccessibly, and where the people who write accessibly pay a small career cost for the choice. Multiplied across thousands of writers and decades of accumulation, the field gets the writing it incentivizes.

That's the structural piece. It's not about villains. It's about a system that rewards a behavior nobody would defend if you asked them about it directly.

Who's already doing it right

Some of the best writers in this space already write accessibly, and it's worth naming them as a pattern even when I'm not naming them by name. The trade journalists who explain rather than credential. The label and distribution executives who publish public memos in plain English. The music lawyers who break down DMCA cases and copyright rulings for readers who aren't lawyers. The royalty operations specialists who write threads explaining why a statement looks the way it looks.

They exist. They're not rare. They're just systematically less visible than the writers who cater to the closed loop, because the loop's amplification mechanisms (panels, podcasts, trade press features) reward the closed register more than the open one.

If you're one of those writers, this Note isn't about you. It's about what I'm trying to learn from you.

What gets lost when the loop wins

When the only audience is insiders, writing stops being clarifying and starts being credentialing. The point of saying "DDEX," "MLC distribution," or "ERN deliveries" stops being to communicate and starts being to signal: I belong in this room. I know the codes.

That's a perfectly human instinct. I've fallen for it more than once. It's also the instinct that quietly excludes everyone the industry exists to serve.

Listeners pay for music. Indirectly through subscriptions, directly through tickets and merchandise, attentionally through every minute they choose to spend with a song instead of something else. They fund the entire system. And almost nothing the industry writes about how the system works is written with them in mind.

Working artists fall in the same gap. Most of them don't know how their own royalty pipeline works, not because they're incurious, but because the writing that explains it is impenetrable. When the writing is impenetrable, the system stays opaque. When the system stays opaque, the people who already understand it stay in charge by default.

The standard I'm trying to hold myself to

Everything I publish on this site uses the same rule now: industry readers can read it, listeners can read it, and I don't care if it reads as overkill for the experts. Acronyms get spelled out. Concepts get defined when needed. The argument doesn't depend on the reader having insider context.

This isn't a marketing tactic. It's a position about who the writing is for.

I don't always get it right. There are pieces I've written where I caught myself reaching for the credentialing word and let it through anyway because it felt natural. The discipline is ongoing. The point is to keep choosing the plain word more often than the coded one, and to forgive yourself when you slip and try again on the next piece.

The invitation

If you're already writing accessibly inside the industry, you're not who this Note is about. You're who I'm trying to learn from.

If you're not, the cost of changing is smaller than it looks. The first time you spell out an acronym in a piece your peers expected you to leave coded, you'll feel like you gave something up. You didn't. You just wrote for a bigger audience than the one you were used to. That audience includes the listener who pays for the system, the artist who's living inside it, and the journalist outside the music vertical who's trying to understand it. All three deserve writing they can follow. The industry didn't lose anything by giving it to them.

The closed loop doesn't end because somebody calls it out from outside. It ends because enough people inside it decide to write for the bigger room. I'm trying to. I hope more of us do.
payload.title: The music industry talks to itself in code
